questions.title: Continuous Normalizing Flow ODE
questions.description: Define the core ODE that governs a continuous normalizing flow (CNF). The state x evolves from t=0 to t=1 via dx/dt = v_theta(x, t).
questions.info: Continuous Normalizing Flows (CNFs), introduced by Chen et al. (2018) in the Neural ODE paper, define a generative model through an ordinary differential equation dx/dt = v_theta(x_t, t). The idea is to learn a velocity field v_theta that transforms a simple prior distribution (e.g., Gaussian) at t=0 into a complex data distribution at t=1. Unlike discrete normalizing flows, CNFs allow free-form architectures since they do not require invertibility by construction. Flow Matching, as proposed by Lipman et al. (2023), provides a simulation-free training objective for these CNFs, avoiding the expensive ODE solves during training.

blanks.hint: The input dimension is x (dim) plus the scalar time t (1).
The velocity field outputs a vector with the same dimension as x.
Concatenate the spatial coordinates x and the time t along the last dimension.

questions.title: Linear Interpolation Path
questions.description: Implement the linear interpolation path x_t = (1 - t) * x_0 + t * x_1, where x_0 is noise and x_1 is data.
questions.info: The linear interpolation path is the simplest conditional probability path used in flow matching. Given a noise sample x_0 ~ N(0, I) and a data sample x_1, the interpolant is x_t = (1 - t) * x_0 + t * x_1. At t=0, x_t = x_0 (pure noise), and at t=1, x_t = x_1 (clean data). This straight-line path was highlighted by Liu et al. (2022) in 'Flow Straight and Fast' (Rectified Flow) as producing straighter trajectories that require fewer ODE integration steps at inference. Lipman et al. (2023) showed this path yields a simple conditional vector field u_t(x|x_1) = x_1 - x_0.

questions.title: Conditional Vector Field
questions.description: Compute the conditional vector field u_t(x|x_1) for the linear interpolation path. For the path x_t = (1-t)*x_0 + t*x_1, the target velocity is simply u_t = x_1 - x_0.
questions.info: For the linear interpolation path x_t = (1 - t)*x_0 + t*x_1, the conditional vector field is obtained by taking the time derivative: dx_t/dt = x_1 - x_0. This is a constant velocity that does not depend on t, meaning each sample travels in a straight line from its noise position x_0 to its data position x_1. This remarkable simplicity is a key advantage of flow matching over diffusion models, as shown by Lipman et al. (2023). The flow matching loss then trains a neural network v_theta(x_t, t) to approximate this target velocity field.

questions.title: Flow Matching Training Loss
questions.description: Implement the flow matching loss: the MSE between the predicted velocity v_theta(x_t, t) and the target velocity u_t = x_1 - x_0.
questions.info: The flow matching (FM) loss is L_FM = E_{t, x_0, x_1}[ || v_theta(x_t, t) - u_t(x_t | x_1) ||^2 ], where t ~ U[0,1], x_0 ~ N(0,I), and x_1 ~ p_data. For the linear interpolation path, u_t = x_1 - x_0. This is a simple regression loss that trains the velocity network to predict the direction from noise to data at each interpolated point. Lipman et al. (2023) proved that minimizing this conditional flow matching (CFM) loss yields the same gradients as the intractable marginal flow matching objective, making training both principled and efficient.

questions.title: Uniform Time Sampling
questions.description: Implement the time sampling strategy for flow matching training. During training, t is sampled uniformly from [0, 1].
questions.info: In flow matching, the time variable t is sampled uniformly from [0, 1] during training: t ~ U(0, 1). This is in contrast to diffusion models where the noise schedule implicitly defines a non-uniform weighting over timesteps. The uniform sampling in flow matching, as described by Lipman et al. (2023) and Liu et al. (2022), treats all points along the interpolation path equally. Some advanced variants like Tong et al. (2023) explore importance sampling of t to focus training on harder timesteps, but the default uniform sampling already yields strong results due to the simplicity of the linear interpolation path.

questions.title: Flow Matching Training Loop
questions.description: Write a complete training step for flow matching: sample noise and time, interpolate, compute the target velocity, predict, and backpropagate the MSE loss.
questions.info: A single training step of flow matching involves: (1) sample a data batch x_1 from the dataset, (2) sample noise x_0 ~ N(0,I), (3) sample t ~ U(0,1), (4) compute x_t = (1-t)*x_0 + t*x_1, (5) compute the target velocity u_t = x_1 - x_0, (6) predict v_theta(x_t, t), and (7) minimize MSE(v_theta, u_t). This procedure from Lipman et al. (2023) is remarkably simple compared to diffusion model training which requires noise schedule design. The loss landscape is also smoother because the target velocity is constant along each conditional path.

questions.title: Gaussian Conditional Probability Path
questions.description: Implement the Gaussian conditional probability path p_t(x|x_1) = N(x; mu_t(x_1), sigma_t^2 I) with mu_t = t*x_1 and sigma_t = 1 - (1 - sigma_min)*t.
questions.info: The Gaussian conditional probability path defines p_t(x|x_1) = N(x; mu_t(x_1), sigma_t^2 I), where mu_t and sigma_t smoothly transition from the prior to the data. In Lipman et al. (2023), the choice mu_t(x_1) = t * x_1 and sigma_t = 1 - (1 - sigma_min) * t ensures that at t=0 we have approximately N(0, I) and at t=1 we have N(x_1, sigma_min^2 I), a tight Gaussian around the data point. The parameter sigma_min > 0 is a small constant (e.g., 1e-4) for numerical stability. Sampling from this path gives x_t = mu_t + sigma_t * epsilon, where epsilon ~ N(0, I).

questions.title: Runge-Kutta 4 ODE Sampler
questions.description: Implement a 4th-order Runge-Kutta (RK4) integrator for more accurate ODE sampling from a trained flow matching model.
questions.info: While the Euler method is simple, it has O(dt) local error. The 4th-order Runge-Kutta (RK4) method achieves O(dt^4) local error, enabling higher-quality samples with fewer steps. RK4 evaluates the velocity field 4 times per step: k1 at (x, t), k2 at (x + dt/2 * k1, t + dt/2), k3 at (x + dt/2 * k2, t + dt/2), k4 at (x + dt * k3, t + dt), then updates x += dt/6 * (k1 + 2*k2 + 2*k3 + k4). For production flow matching models, adaptive-step solvers from torchdiffeq are common, but fixed-step RK4 is a good middle ground between accuracy and simplicity.
questions.code:
import torch

@torch.no_grad()
def rk4_sample(model, shape, num_steps=50, device='cpu'):
    """Sample using RK4 integration."""
    x = torch.randn(shape, device=device)
    dt = 1.0 / num_steps

    for i in range(num_steps):
        t_cur = i / num_steps
        t_batch = torch.full((shape[0],), t_cur, device=device)
        t_mid = torch.full((shape[0],), t_cur + 0.5 * dt, device=device)
        t_end = torch.full((shape[0],), t_cur + dt, device=device)

        k1 = model(x, t_batch)
        k2 = model(x + 0.5 * dt * k1, t_mid)
        k3 = model({{b1}}, t_mid)
        k4 = model({{b2}}, t_end)

        x = x + {{b3}}

    return x

k4 is evaluated at x + dt * k3, a full step using k3's slope.

questions.title: Flow Matching vs Diffusion: Loss Comparison
questions.description: Implement both the flow matching loss and the diffusion (DDPM) loss side by side to see their structural similarity and differences.
questions.info: Flow matching and diffusion models are closely related but differ in their training objectives. DDPM trains a network epsilon_theta(x_t, t) to predict the added noise, with x_t = sqrt(alpha_t) * x_1 + sqrt(1 - alpha_t) * epsilon. Flow matching trains v_theta(x_t, t) to predict the velocity x_1 - x_0, with x_t = (1-t)*x_0 + t*x_1. Lipman et al. (2023) showed that flow matching's loss landscape is simpler because the target velocity is constant (independent of t) for each (x_0, x_1) pair, whereas diffusion's target depends on the noise schedule. Both are MSE losses but flow matching avoids the need for a carefully designed noise schedule.

The flow matching target is the constant velocity x_1 - x_0.
Compute MSE between the predicted velocity and the target velocity.

questions.title: Log-Likelihood via Instantaneous Change of Variables
questions.description: Compute the log-likelihood of data under a CNF using the instantaneous change of variables formula involving the trace of the Jacobian.
questions.info: CNFs can compute exact log-likelihoods using the instantaneous change of variables formula: d/dt log p_t(x_t) = -Tr(dv_theta/dx_t). This is derived from the continuity equation and was introduced by Chen et al. (2018). To evaluate log p_1(x_1), one integrates backward from t=1 to t=0 while accumulating the trace of the Jacobian of v_theta. The Hutchinson trace estimator Tr(J) approx= epsilon^T J epsilon (where epsilon ~ N(0,I)) makes this tractable without computing the full Jacobian. This gives flow matching models the ability to perform density estimation, unlike most diffusion models.
questions.code:
import torch

def compute_log_prob(model, x_1, num_steps=100):
    """Compute log p(x_1) using change of variables.
    Integrates backward from t=1 to t=0."""
    x = x_1.clone().requires_grad_(True)
    log_prob = torch.zeros(x.shape[0], device=x.device)
    dt = -1.0 / num_steps  # negative because we go backward

    for i in range(num_steps):
        t_val = 1.0 - i / num_steps
        t = torch.full((x.shape[0],), t_val, device=x.device)

        # Compute velocity and its divergence using Hutchinson estimator
        epsilon = torch.randn_like(x)
        with torch.enable_grad():
            x_in = x.detach().requires_grad_(True)
            v = model(x_in, t)
            # Hutchinson trace estimator: Tr(J) ~ eps^T @ J @ eps
            jvp = torch.autograd.grad(
                outputs={{b1}},
                inputs=x_in,
                create_graph=False
            )[0]
            div_v = {{b2}}

        x = x + dt * v.detach()
        log_prob = {{b3}}

    # Add log p_0(x) = log N(x; 0, I)
    log_prob += -0.5 * (x ** 2).sum(-1) - 0.5 * x.shape[-1] * torch.log(torch.tensor(2 * 3.14159265))
    return log_prob

The Hutchinson estimator is eps^T @ (J @ eps) = (jvp * epsilon).sum(dim=-1).

questions.title: Variance-Preserving Interpolation Path
questions.description: Implement the variance-preserving (VP) interpolation path: x_t = cos(pi*t/2)*x_0 + sin(pi*t/2)*x_1, which is closely related to VP diffusion.
questions.info: The variance-preserving (VP) path uses trigonometric interpolation: x_t = cos(pi*t/2)*x_0 + sin(pi*t/2)*x_1. When x_0 ~ N(0,I), this ensures Var(x_t) = I for all t (since cos^2 + sin^2 = 1), matching the VP SDE used in score-based diffusion models (Song et al., 2021). The corresponding conditional vector field is u_t = (pi/2)*(-sin(pi*t/2)*x_0 + cos(pi*t/2)*x_1). Lipman et al. (2023) showed that flow matching can use any differentiable interpolation path, and the VP path connects flow matching to the diffusion model literature, providing a unified perspective.

import torch
import math

def vp_interpolation(x_0, x_1, t):
    """Variance-preserving interpolation path.
    Args:
        x_0: noise, shape (batch, dim)
        x_1: data, shape (batch, dim)
        t: time, shape (batch, 1)
    Returns:
        x_t: interpolated point
        u_t: conditional vector field
    """
    angle = {{b1}}
    x_t = torch.cos(angle) * x_0 + torch.sin(angle) * x_1

    # Conditional velocity: d(x_t)/dt
    u_t = {{b2}} * (-torch.sin(angle) * x_0 + {{b3}} * x_1)
    return x_t, u_t

questions.title: Exponential Moving Average for Flow Models

questions.info: Exponential Moving Average (EMA) of model parameters is a standard technique used in both diffusion and flow matching models to improve generation quality. The EMA parameters are updated as theta_ema = decay * theta_ema + (1 - decay) * theta, where decay is typically 0.999 or 0.9999. The EMA model is only used at inference time, not during training. This smooths out noisy parameter updates and was shown by Song & Ermon (2020) and Lipman et al. (2023) to significantly improve sample quality. The EMA model tracks a running average without requiring additional gradient computation.

questions.title: Complete Flow Matching Pipeline
questions.description: Assemble a complete flow matching pipeline: model creation, training loop with data, and sampling. Fill in the key missing components.
questions.info: A complete flow matching pipeline consists of three phases: (1) model definition -- a neural network v_theta(x, t) that maps (x_t, t) to a velocity vector, (2) training -- minimizing E[||v_theta(x_t, t) - (x_1 - x_0)||^2] over minibatches with t ~ U(0,1), and (3) sampling -- integrating dx = v_theta(x, t) dt from t=0 to t=1 starting from x_0 ~ N(0,I). Lipman et al. (2023) showed this pipeline achieves competitive results with diffusion models while being simpler to implement. The entire training requires no noise schedule, no special weighting, and no score function estimation.
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

# 1. Define model
model = nn.Sequential(
    nn.Linear(3, 256), nn.SiLU(),
    nn.Linear(256, 256), nn.SiLU(),
    nn.Linear(256, 2)
)
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)

def predict(x, t):
    return model(torch.cat([x, t.unsqueeze(-1)], dim=-1))

# 2. Training (one epoch)
for step in range(1000):
    x_1 = sample_data(batch_size=256)    # data
    x_0 = torch.randn_like(x_1)          # noise
    t = torch.rand(256)
    t_e = t.unsqueeze(-1)
    x_t = (1 - t_e) * x_0 + t_e * x_1
    loss = F.mse_loss(predict(x_t, t), {{b1}})
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()

# 3. Sampling
with torch.no_grad():
    x = {{b2}}
    for i in range(100):
        t = torch.full((512,), {{b3}})
        x = x + 0.01 * predict(x, t)

blanks.hint: The flow matching target is the velocity from noise to data: x_1 - x_0.
Start sampling from pure Gaussian noise with 512 samples of dimension 2.
The current time t at step i with 100 total steps is i/100.

questions.title: Class-Conditional Flow Matching
questions.description: Extend flow matching to class-conditional generation by embedding class labels and feeding them to the velocity network alongside x and t.
questions.info: Class-conditional flow matching extends the velocity field to v_theta(x_t, t, y), where y is a class label. This is analogous to class-conditional diffusion models. The class label is typically embedded via a learned embedding table and added to or concatenated with the time embedding. During training, the class label is sometimes randomly dropped (set to a null token) with some probability (e.g., 10%) to enable classifier-free guidance at inference. Lipman et al. (2023) note that flow matching naturally supports conditioning without modification to the training objective -- one simply adds the conditioning information to the network input.

questions.title: Classifier-Free Guidance for Flow Matching
questions.description: Implement classifier-free guidance (CFG) during sampling from a conditional flow matching model. The guided velocity is v_guided = (1 + w)*v(x,t,y) - w*v(x,t,null).
questions.info: Classifier-free guidance (CFG), introduced by Ho & Salimans (2022) for diffusion models, applies directly to flow matching. During training, the class label is randomly replaced with a null token with probability p_uncond (e.g., 0.1). At inference, the guided velocity is v_guided = v_uncond + w * (v_cond - v_uncond) = (1 + w) * v_cond - w * v_uncond, where w is the guidance scale. Higher w produces samples more tightly aligned with the conditioning but with less diversity. This technique was adopted for flow matching by Lipman et al. (2023) and is standard in text-to-image flow models like Stable Diffusion 3 (Esser et al., 2024).
questions.code:
import torch

@torch.no_grad()
def cfg_sample(model, shape, class_label, null_label, guidance_scale=3.0,
               num_steps=100, device='cpu'):
    """Sample with classifier-free guidance."""
    x = torch.randn(shape, device=device)
    dt = 1.0 / num_steps

    for i in range(num_steps):
        t = torch.full((shape[0],), i / num_steps, device=device)

        # Conditional prediction
        v_cond = model(x, t, class_label)
        # Unconditional prediction
        v_uncond = model(x, t, {{b1}})

        # Guided velocity
        v_guided = {{b2}}

        x = x + {{b3}}

    return x

questions.title: Affine Interpolation Path
questions.description: Implement the general affine interpolation path x_t = alpha_t * x_1 + beta_t * x_0, where alpha_t and beta_t are time-dependent scalar schedules with alpha_0=0, alpha_1=1, beta_0=1, beta_1=0.
questions.info: The affine interpolation path is the most general linear interpolation between noise x_0 and data x_1 in flow matching. The simplest choice is the linear or optimal transport path with alpha_t = t and beta_t = 1 - t. Different schedule choices lead to different conditional velocity fields and can affect training stability and sample quality.

questions.title: Spherical Linear Interpolation (Slerp)
questions.description: Implement spherical linear interpolation (slerp) between two points on the unit sphere. Slerp interpolates along the great circle arc, preserving the norm of the interpolated vector throughout the path.
questions.info: Spherical linear interpolation (slerp) is used in flow matching for high-dimensional data where preserving vector norms during interpolation is important, such as in image generation and 3D applications. Unlike linear interpolation which cuts through the interior of the sphere, slerp traverses the great circle arc. The formula is slerp(x_0, x_1, t) = sin((1-t)*omega)/sin(omega) * x_0 + sin(t*omega)/sin(omega) * x_1, where omega is the angle between x_0 and x_1. This is particularly useful when working with normalized embeddings or latent representations.
questions.code:
import torch

def slerp(x_0: torch.Tensor, x_1: torch.Tensor, t: torch.Tensor, eps: float = 1e-6):
    """Spherical linear interpolation between x_0 and x_1.
    
    Args:
        x_0: starting points, shape (B, D)
        x_1: ending points, shape (B, D)
        t: interpolation parameter in [0,1], shape (B, 1)
        eps: small value for numerical stability
    Returns:
        x_t: slerp-interpolated points, shape (B, D)
    """
    # Compute the cosine of the angle between x_0 and x_1
    x_0_norm = x_0 / (x_0.norm(dim=-1, keepdim=True) + eps)
    x_1_norm = x_1 / (x_1.norm(dim=-1, keepdim=True) + eps)
    cos_omega = (x_0_norm * x_1_norm).sum(dim=-1, keepdim=True).clamp(-1, 1)
    omega = {{b1}}
    sin_omega = torch.sin(omega) + eps
    
    # Slerp formula
    coeff_0 = {{b2}} / sin_omega
    coeff_1 = {{b3}} / sin_omega
    
    x_t = coeff_0 * x_0 + coeff_1 * x_1
    return x_t

blanks.hint: Compute the angle omega from cos(omega) using the inverse cosine function.
The coefficient for x_0 in slerp is sin((1-t)*omega).
The coefficient for x_1 in slerp is sin(t*omega).

questions.title: Noise-Augmented Interpolation (Sigma-Augmented CFM)
questions.description: Implement noise-augmented (sigma-augmented) interpolation for Conditional Flow Matching, where Gaussian noise with standard deviation sigma_t is added to the interpolation path: x_t = alpha_t * x_1 + beta_t * x_0 + sigma_t * epsilon.
questions.info: Sigma-augmented Conditional Flow Matching adds a small Gaussian perturbation to the interpolation path, resulting in x_t = alpha_t * x_1 + beta_t * x_0 + sigma_t * epsilon. This augmentation ensures that the conditional probability paths have full support (are non-degenerate Gaussians), which is required for the theoretical guarantees of flow matching. The noise schedule sigma_t is typically small and goes to zero at t=0 and t=1, ensuring the endpoints remain clean. A common choice is sigma_t = sigma_min * t * (1-t), which peaks at t=0.5.

questions.title: Independent Coupling for Flow Matching
questions.description: Implement independent coupling where noise x_0 and data x_1 are sampled independently from their respective marginals. This is the simplest coupling strategy used in standard flow matching.
questions.info: Independent coupling is the default coupling strategy in flow matching: noise samples x_0 are drawn from the prior distribution (usually a standard Gaussian) independently of the data samples x_1. This means there is no correspondence between specific noise samples and data samples within a mini-batch. While simple and easy to implement, independent coupling can lead to crossing trajectories when the transport map is complex, making the velocity field harder to learn. More sophisticated coupling strategies like mini-batch OT can reduce trajectory crossings.

questions.title: Mini-Batch Optimal Transport Coupling
questions.description: Implement mini-batch optimal transport (OT) coupling using the cost matrix and the scipy linear_sum_assignment solver. This reorders noise samples to minimize the total transport cost within each mini-batch.
questions.info: Mini-batch OT coupling, introduced in OT-CFM by Tong et al. (2023), pairs noise and data samples within a mini-batch to minimize the total squared Euclidean transport cost. Instead of independent random pairing, we compute the pairwise cost matrix C[i,j] = ||x_0^i - x_1^j||^2 and solve a linear assignment problem. This produces straighter, non-crossing conditional trajectories that are easier for the velocity network to learn, leading to faster convergence and better sample quality. The per-batch OT assignment is a practical approximation of the full OT coupling between distributions.

questions.title: Exact OT Coupling in 1D via Sorting
questions.description: Implement the exact optimal transport coupling in 1D by sorting. In one dimension, the OT map is the monotone rearrangement: sort both distributions and match them by rank.
questions.info: In one dimension, the optimal transport map between two distributions is uniquely given by the monotone rearrangement: sort both the source and target samples and pair them by their rank order. This is an exact solution to the Monge problem in 1D and runs in O(n log n) time due to sorting. For higher dimensions, exact OT is computationally expensive (cubic in the number of samples), which is why mini-batch OT or entropic regularization are used instead. The 1D case is important as a building block for sliced OT methods.

questions.title: Action Matching Loss
questions.description: Implement the action matching objective, which matches the kinetic energy (action) along interpolation paths. The action is the integral of ||v||^2 over time, and action matching provides an alternative to velocity regression.
questions.info: Action matching, proposed by Neklyudov et al. (2023), provides an alternative training objective for continuous normalizing flows. Instead of regressing onto the conditional velocity field, it minimizes the kinetic energy (action) functional: integral of ||v_theta(x_t, t)||^2 dt along the trajectory. The key insight is that the model with minimal action that still matches the marginals is the optimal transport velocity field. The practical loss involves a gradient term computed via automatic differentiation, making it more related to score matching than velocity regression.
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

def action_matching_loss(
    model: nn.Module, x_0: torch.Tensor, x_1: torch.Tensor, t: torch.Tensor
):
    """Compute action matching loss.
    
    The loss has two terms:
    1. Kinetic energy: ||v_theta(x_t, t)||^2
    2. A correction term involving the divergence of v_theta
    
    Args:
        model: velocity field v_theta(x, t)
        x_0: noise, shape (B, D)
        x_1: data, shape (B, D)
        t: time, shape (B, 1)
    Returns:
        loss: scalar action matching loss
    """
    # Interpolate
    x_t = (1 - t) * x_0 + t * x_1
    x_t.requires_grad_(True)
    
    # Predict velocity
    v = model(x_t, t.squeeze(-1))
    
    # Kinetic energy term
    kinetic = {{b1}}
    
    # Compute divergence via Hutchinson trace estimator
    eps = torch.randn_like(x_t)
    v_eps = {{b2}}
    grad_v_eps = torch.autograd.grad(v_eps, x_t, create_graph=True)[0]
    div_v = {{b3}}
    
    # Action matching loss = kinetic energy + divergence
    loss = (kinetic + div_v).mean()
    return loss

For the Hutchinson estimator, take the dot product of v with random epsilon.
The Hutchinson trace estimator: tr(Jv) = E[eps^T * Jv * eps].

questions.title: Flow Matching for Images with UNet
questions.description: Set up a flow matching training step for image generation using a UNet architecture. The velocity field takes a noisy image and a timestep as input and predicts the velocity to transform noise into a clean image.
questions.info: Flow matching for images follows the same principle as for low-dimensional data, but uses convolutional architectures like UNets to parameterize the velocity field. The UNet takes a noisy image x_t and a timestep embedding as input and predicts the velocity field v_theta(x_t, t). Images are typically normalized to [-1, 1] before training. The interpolation path is the same: x_t = (1-t)*x_0 + t*x_1, where x_0 is Gaussian noise and x_1 is a real image. The target velocity for the OT path is simply u_t = x_1 - x_0.

questions.title: Flow Matching for 3D Point Clouds
questions.description: Implement a flow matching training step for 3D point cloud generation. Each point cloud is a set of N 3D points, and the velocity field operates on the full point cloud using a simple PointNet-like architecture.
questions.info: Flow matching can generate 3D point clouds by learning to transform a Gaussian noise point cloud into a structured 3D shape. Each sample is a set of N points in R^3, represented as a tensor of shape (N, 3). The velocity field v_theta takes the entire noisy point cloud and time as input. A key design choice is permutation equivariance: since point clouds are unordered sets, the architecture should treat points symmetrically. PointNet-style architectures with shared MLPs and global pooling naturally satisfy this requirement.
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class PointCloudVelocity(nn.Module):
    """Simple PointNet-like velocity field for point clouds."""
    def __init__(self, hidden=128):
        super().__init__()
        # Per-point MLP (shared weights across points)
        self.point_net = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear({{b1}}, hidden),  # 3 coords + 1 time
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden, hidden)
        )
        # After global pooling, combine with per-point features
        self.output_net = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(hidden * 2, hidden),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden, 3)  # output 3D velocity
        )
    
    def forward(self, points, t):
        """Args: points (B, N, 3), t (B,) -> velocities (B, N, 3)"""
        B, N, _ = points.shape
        t_expand = t.view(B, 1, 1).expand(B, N, 1)
        x_in = torch.cat([points, t_expand], dim=-1)
        
        # Per-point features
        h = self.point_net(x_in)  # (B, N, hidden)
        
        # Global feature via max pooling over points
        global_feat = {{b2}}  # (B, hidden)
        global_feat = global_feat.unsqueeze(1).expand(B, N, -1)  # (B, N, hidden)
        
        # Concatenate per-point and global features
        combined = {{b3}}
        return self.output_net(combined)

questions.title: Time-Dependent Velocity Field with Sinusoidal Embedding
questions.description: Implement a time-dependent velocity field where the scalar time t is embedded using sinusoidal positional encoding (similar to transformers) before being fed into the network.
questions.info: Sinusoidal time embeddings, borrowed from transformer positional encodings and diffusion models, provide a rich representation of the scalar time value. The embedding maps t to a vector using sine and cosine functions at different frequencies: [sin(omega_1*t), cos(omega_1*t), ..., sin(omega_k*t), cos(omega_k*t)]. The frequencies are typically geometric: omega_i = 1/10000^(2i/d). This allows the network to distinguish between nearby time values more easily than a raw scalar input, improving the quality of the learned velocity field.

questions.title: Exact Divergence of a Velocity Field
questions.description: Compute the exact divergence of a velocity field v_theta(x, t) using torch.autograd. The divergence div(v) = sum_i dv_i/dx_i is the trace of the Jacobian.
questions.info: The divergence of the velocity field is crucial for computing log-likelihoods via the instantaneous change of variables formula: d/dt log p(x_t) = -div(v_theta(x_t, t)). Computing the exact divergence requires D backward passes (one per dimension) to get all diagonal elements of the Jacobian. This costs O(D) in compute, which is expensive for high-dimensional data. The Hutchinson trace estimator provides a stochastic O(1) alternative. The exact divergence is useful for low-dimensional problems or as a validation tool.
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

def exact_divergence(v_fn: nn.Module, x: torch.Tensor, t: torch.Tensor):
    """Compute exact divergence of v_fn at (x, t).
    
    div(v) = sum_i dv_i/dx_i (trace of the Jacobian)
    
    Args:
        v_fn: velocity field module
        x: points, shape (B, D), requires_grad=True
        t: times, shape (B,)
    Returns:
        div_v: divergence values, shape (B,)
    """
    x = x.requires_grad_(True)
    v = v_fn(x, t)  # (B, D)
    D = x.shape[1]
    
    div_v = torch.zeros(x.shape[0], device=x.device)
    for i in range(D):
        # Compute dv_i / dx_i
        grad_i = torch.autograd.grad(
            outputs={{b1}},
            inputs=x,
            grad_outputs={{b2}},
            create_graph=True,
            retain_graph=True
        )[0]
        div_v += {{b3}}
    
    return div_v

questions.title: Hutchinson Trace Estimator for Divergence
questions.description: Implement the Hutchinson stochastic trace estimator for computing the divergence of a velocity field in O(1) instead of O(D). This uses the identity: tr(J) = E[eps^T J eps] where eps is a random vector.
questions.info: The Hutchinson trace estimator provides an unbiased estimate of the trace of a matrix A via tr(A) = E[eps^T A eps], where eps is drawn from a distribution with E[eps eps^T] = I (e.g., standard Gaussian or Rademacher). For computing the divergence of a velocity field, this avoids the O(D) cost of computing all diagonal Jacobian entries. The key trick is that eps^T J eps = eps^T * grad(v . eps, x), which requires only a single backward pass. This is essential for density estimation with CNFs in high dimensions.
questions.code:

    Args:
        v_fn: velocity field module
        x: points, shape (B, D), will be set to require grad
        t: times, shape (B,)
    Returns:
        div_estimate: estimated divergence, shape (B,)

    v = v_fn(x, t)  # (B, D)

    eps = {{b1}}
    
    # Compute v . eps (scalar per batch element)
    v_dot_eps = {{b2}}
    
    # Compute gradient of (v . eps) w.r.t. x
    grad_v_eps = torch.autograd.grad(

questions.title: Log-Determinant of Jacobian via ODE
questions.description: Implement the joint ODE that simultaneously evolves the state x_t and accumulates the log-determinant of the Jacobian for density estimation. This uses the instantaneous change of variables formula.
questions.info: The instantaneous change of variables formula states that d/dt log p(x_t) = -div(v(x_t, t)), which means the log-density changes at a rate equal to the negative divergence of the velocity field. To compute the log-likelihood of a data point, we integrate this ODE jointly with the state ODE: dx/dt = v(x,t) and d(log_det)/dt = -div(v(x,t)). Starting from data x_1 at t=1, we integrate backward to t=0 to get the corresponding noise z and the accumulated log-det, then evaluate log p(x_1) = log p_0(z) + log_det.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

def joint_ode_step(v_fn, x, log_det, t, dt, div_fn):
    """One Euler step of the joint state + log-det ODE.
    
    dx/dt = v(x, t)
    d(log_det)/dt = -div(v(x, t))
    
    Args:
        v_fn: velocity field
        x: current state, shape (B, D)
        log_det: accumulated log-det, shape (B,)
        t: current time (scalar)
        dt: step size (scalar, negative for backward integration)
        div_fn: function to compute divergence
    Returns:
        x_new, log_det_new
    """
    t_tensor = torch.full((x.shape[0],), t, device=x.device)
    
    # Velocity
    v = v_fn(x, t_tensor)
    
    # Divergence
    div_v = div_fn(v_fn, x, t_tensor)
    
    # Update state
    x_new = {{b1}}
    
    # Update log-determinant
    log_det_new = {{b2}}
    
    return x_new, log_det_new

def compute_log_likelihood(v_fn, x_1, div_fn, num_steps=100):
    """Compute log p(x_1) by integrating from t=1 to t=0."""
    dt = -1.0 / num_steps
    x = x_1.clone()
    log_det = torch.zeros(x.shape[0], device=x.device)
    
    for i in range(num_steps):
        t = 1.0 + i * dt
        x, log_det = joint_ode_step(v_fn, x, log_det, t, dt, div_fn)
    
    # x is now approximately x_0 ~ N(0, I)
    log_p0 = {{b3}}
    return log_p0 + log_det

The key property is that this transform is trivially invertible and has a triangular Jacobian, so its log-determinant is simply the sum of the scale factors s. class AffineCouplingLayer(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, dim: int, hidden: int = 128):

        self.half_dim = dim // 2
        # Network predicts scale (s) and translation (t)
        self.net = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(self.half_dim, hidden),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden, hidden),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden, self.half_dim * 2)  # outputs [s, t]

        st = self.net(x_a)
        s, t = st[:, :self.half_dim], st[:, self.half_dim:]
        
        # Affine transform on x_b
        y_b = {{b1}}
        log_det = {{b2}}
        
        y = torch.cat([x_a, y_b], dim=-1)
        return y, log_det
    
    def inverse(self, y):
        """Inverse pass: y -> x."""
        y_a, y_b = y[:, :self.half_dim], y[:, self.half_dim:]
        st = self.net(y_a)
        s, t = st[:, :self.half_dim], st[:, self.half_dim:]

        x = torch.cat([y_a, x_b], dim=-1)

The error is approximately ||x_full - x_half||, and the step is accepted if error < tolerance. import torch

def adaptive_euler_sample(
    model, x_init, tol=1e-3, dt_init=0.1, dt_min=1e-4, dt_max=0.5
):
    """Adaptive step size Euler ODE sampler.
    
    Args:
        model: velocity field v(x, t)
        x_init: initial noise, shape (B, D)
        tol: error tolerance
    Returns:
        x: final samples, shape (B, D)
        total_nfe: number of function evaluations used
    """
    x = x_init.clone()
    t = 0.0
    dt = dt_init
    total_nfe = 0
    
    while t < 1.0 - 1e-6:
        dt = min(dt, 1.0 - t)  # don't overshoot
        t_tensor = torch.full((x.shape[0],), t, device=x.device)
        
        # Full step
        v1 = model(x, t_tensor)
        x_full = x + dt * v1
        total_nfe += 1
        
        # Two half steps
        x_half = x + (dt / 2) * v1
        t_mid = torch.full((x.shape[0],), t + dt / 2, device=x.device)
        v2 = model(x_half, t_mid)
        x_half = {{b1}}
        total_nfe += 1
        
        # Error estimate
        error = {{b2}}
        
        if error < tol:
            # Accept step, use the more accurate estimate
            x = x_half
            t += dt
            # Try increasing step size
            dt = min(dt * {{b3}}, dt_max)
        else:
            # Reject and reduce step size
            dt = max(dt * 0.5, dt_min)
    
    return x, total_nfe

questions.title: Learning Rate Scheduler for Flow Matching Training
questions.description: Implement a warmup-then-cosine learning rate scheduler commonly used for training flow matching models. The scheduler linearly warms up for a number of steps, then applies cosine decay.
questions.info: Training flow matching models typically requires careful learning rate scheduling. A common strategy is linear warmup followed by cosine annealing: the learning rate increases linearly from 0 to lr_max over warmup_steps, then decays following a cosine curve to lr_min. This prevents early training instability from large gradients and ensures smooth convergence. The warmup phase is especially important for flow matching since the velocity field must learn to denoise at all time steps simultaneously.

questions.title: Comparing Different Interpolation Paths
questions.description: Implement and compare three different interpolation paths for flow matching: linear (OT), variance-preserving (VP), and variance-exploding (VE). Visualize how they differ in trajectory shape.
questions.info: Different interpolation paths lead to different conditional velocity fields and training dynamics. The linear/OT path x_t = (1-t)*x_0 + t*x_1 produces straight trajectories with constant velocity. The VP path x_t = cos(pi*t/2)*x_0 + sin(pi*t/2)*x_1 preserves variance throughout, connecting to VP diffusion. The VE path x_t = x_1 + (1-t)*x_0 keeps the data signal and gradually removes noise. Each path has a different conditional velocity field and may perform differently depending on the data distribution and architecture.

blanks.hint: VP path: x_t = cos(pi*t/2)*x_0 + sin(pi*t/2)*x_1.
VE path: data x_1 plus decaying noise (1-t)*x_0.
Derivative of sin is cos: d/dt sin(pi*t/2) = (pi/2)*cos(pi*t/2).

questions.title: OT-CFM Training Loss with Mini-Batch OT
questions.description: Implement a complete OT-CFM training step that first computes mini-batch OT coupling, then performs the flow matching loss with the optimally paired samples.
questions.info: OT-CFM (Optimal Transport Conditional Flow Matching) by Tong et al. (2023) combines mini-batch OT coupling with the flow matching loss. First, the noise-data pairs are optimally matched within each mini-batch using linear assignment on the squared Euclidean cost. Then, the standard flow matching loss is computed using these OT-coupled pairs. The OT coupling produces straighter conditional trajectories that are closer to the true OT displacement map, leading to faster training convergence and better sample quality, especially for complex multi-modal distributions.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from scipy.optimize import linear_sum_assignment

def ot_cfm_training_step(
    model: nn.Module, x_1: torch.Tensor, optimizer: torch.optim.Optimizer
):
    """One training step of OT-CFM.
    
    Args:
        model: velocity field v_theta(x, t)
        x_1: data batch, shape (B, D)
        optimizer: optimizer
    Returns:
        loss value (float)
    """
    B, D = x_1.shape
    
    # Step 1: Sample noise
    x_0 = torch.randn_like(x_1)
    
    # Step 2: Compute mini-batch OT coupling
    with torch.no_grad():
        cost = torch.cdist(x_0, x_1).pow(2)
        _, col_idx = linear_sum_assignment(cost.cpu().numpy())
        x_0 = x_0[col_idx]
    
    # Step 3: Sample time and interpolate

    x_t = (1 - t) * x_0 + t * x_1
